text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma (Clear Cell, Sarcomatoid Features)

**ATTENDING PHYSICIAN:** [Attending Oncologist]
**FELLOW/RESIDENT:** [Fellow]
**VISIT TYPE:** Established patient follow-up, restaging review

---

**CHIEF COMPLAINT:**
Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with metastatic clear cell RCC (right kidney, pT3a pN1 M1, sarcomatoid differentiation ~20%, ISUP grade 3) presenting for scheduled oncology follow-up. She is approximately 18 months from her original diagnosis and has been on cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg daily for approximately 9 months. Today we are reviewing restaging CT results and performing comprehensive toxicity/disease assessment.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:**
Ms. Okonkwo was initially diagnosed with stage IV ccRCC in the setting of a large 11.4 cm right renal mass with retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary metastases. The tumor had sarcomatoid differentiation (~20%), ISUP grade 3, and PD-L1 CPS of 15-18 on surgical specimen. NGS (FoundationOne CDx) demonstrated the canonical VHL loss, as well as loss-of-function alterations in PBRM1, BAP1, and SETD2, plus a TP53 hotspot (p.R248W) — a genomic profile consistent with aggressive ccRCC, with BAP1 co-loss felt to partially explain the sarcomatoid phenotype.

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with RPLND approximately 18 months ago (5/8 nodes positive, extranodal extension from the largest at 2.8 cm). Following recovery, she initiated first-line ipi/nivo (Checkmate 214 regimen, ipi 1 mg/kg + nivo 3 mg/kg q3w x 4 cycles) about 6 weeks post-op. She completed 4 induction cycles with a mixed/partial response — dominant RLL lesion shrank ~44%, some other nodules stable. She was transitioned to nivo 480 mg flat dose q4w maintenance. During induction she developed grade 2 immune colitis (prednisone taper, ipi held one cycle, resolved) and subsequently grade 2 hypothyroidism (~5 months in, levothyroxine 75 mcg initiated).

At approximately 9 months on IO, restaging CT showed clear progression: all pulmonary nodules enlarging, two new right middle lobe nodules, and new bilateral hepatic lesions (2 in the right lobe, 0.8 and 0.6 cm). Nivo was discontinued and she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib 60 mg daily.

Cabozantinib was dose-reduced to 40 mg at ~3 weeks for grade 2 hand-foot syndrome. Additionally, she had grade 2 diarrhea requiring brief 5-day hold and loperamide, and grade 2 hypertension requiring amlodipine uptitration to 10 mg. On 40 mg, toxicities have largely stabilized. Restaging at 3 months showed minor decreases in pulmonary targets with hepatic lesions stable — overall stable disease. Restaging at 6 months confirmed continued stable disease across all sites. Today marks approximately 9 months on cabo.

Interval history: She reports persistent but manageable fatigue — she describes it as feeling "like I'm always running on half a tank." She notes some ongoing difficulty walking longer distances secondary to mild residual plantar tenderness (right foot more than left). Diarrhea is currently at 1-2 loose stools per day above her pre-treatment baseline, manageable with dietary adjustments and occasional loperamide. No nausea, no vomiting. No oral mucositis currently. Blood pressure has been running in the 130s/80s at home on amlodipine 10 mg. No chest pain, no shortness of breath, no hemoptysis. No new neurological symptoms. No fever or signs of infection. Weight down approximately 4 kg from her pre-cabo baseline, which has been stable over the last 3 months — no further active weight loss reported.

She denies any new bone pain or headaches. No abdominal pain. She has adapted her daily routine and continues to be functional at home. She lives with her adult daughter who assists with some household tasks. She has been engaged with integrative oncology referral (exercise counseling), which she finds beneficial for mood and energy. Social work has been following her for psychosocial support — she has expressed some anxiety around the possibility of future progression.

Palliative care was discussed at the 6-month visit; she declined at that time but today expresses willingness to at least speak with the team for supportive care optimization, particularly around fatigue and pain management.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:**
- Constitutional: Moderate fatigue (ongoing, stable from prior visit). No fever, no night sweats. Stable appetite, no active weight loss in last 3 months.
- HEENT: No headache, no visual changes.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with brisk walking, unchanged from prior. No hemoptysis, no cough.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations. Blood pressure monitored at home.
- GI: 1-2 loose stools/day above baseline (Grade 1, stable). No hematochezia, no rectal bleeding. No cramping. No nausea/vomiting.
- GU: Functioning with single left kidney. No dysuria, no gross hematuria.
- MSK/Derm: Bilateral plantar tenderness, Grade 1 (right > left). Mild erythema bilateral palms, no active blistering or peeling. No new skin lesions.
- Neuro: No new neurological symptoms.
- Endocrine: Taking levothyroxine — no symptoms of hypo or hyperthyroidism currently.
- Psych: Anxiety around disease, engaged with social work support.

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:**
- General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Speaks clearly.
- Vital Signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, O2 sat 97% on room air, Weight 68 kg (down ~4 kg from pre-TKI baseline, stable x 3 months).
- HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx without mucositis or ulceration today. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy appreciated.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
- Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, no crackles.
- Abdomen: Soft, nontender. Well-healed right flank nephrectomy scar. No hepatosplenomegaly palpated. No palpable abdominal masses.
- Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Bilateral plantar surfaces — mild erythema with hyperkeratosis on heels and lateral plantar surface bilaterally (right more pronounced), no frank ulceration or blistering. Bilateral palmar surfaces — mild diffuse erythema with slight skin thickening at palmar creases, no peeling or vesiculation currently.
- Neuro: Non-focal. Gait mildly antalgic due to plantar tenderness.
- Lymph nodes: No peripheral adenopathy on exam.

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY):**
- CBC: WBC 5.8 k/uL, Hgb 10.8 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable), Plt 198 k/uL
- BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 22, Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (stable, baseline post-nephrectomy ~1.3-1.4), Glucose 94
- LFTs: AST 28, ALT 32, AlkPhos 84, Total Bili 0.6 — all within normal limits
- TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- Urinalysis: trace protein, no hematuria (expected with single kidney)
- PT/INR: Not checked today
- Lipase: Not checked today
- Urine protein:creatinine ratio: Pending (ordered today given ongoing TKI therapy — prior check 3 months ago was 0.21, acceptable)

---

**IMAGING RESULTS (REVIEWED TODAY):**
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast — most recent restaging scan (obtained within the past week):

Chest: Right lower lobe lesion is again seen, minimally changed compared with the prior scan — stable in appearance. Left upper lobe nodule remains present, no appreciable change. Right upper lobe and left lower lobe nodules are similarly stable. The right middle lobe nodules are essentially unchanged. No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No pleural effusion. No hilar or mediastinal adenopathy.

Abdomen/Pelvis: Right hepatic lobe lesions are again noted — the larger measuring approximately 0.7 cm and the smaller approximately 0.5 cm, stable from prior exam. No new hepatic lesions. No biliary dilation. Nephrectomy bed appears clean without evidence of local recurrence. No new retroperitoneal adenopathy. Adrenal glands unremarkable (left in place; right was preserved at time of surgery). No ascites.

Bone windows: No new lytic or sclerotic lesions. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, unchanged.

Overall impression: All sites of known disease are stable compared with the prior restaging scan. No new lesions identified. Continued disease control on cabozantinib.

---

**ASSESSMENT:**
1. Metastatic ccRCC (pT3a pN1 M1, sarcomatoid 20%, ISUP grade 3) — continued stable disease on cabozantinib 40 mg daily (9 months on cabo, ~18 months from diagnosis). Durable disease control noted across all sites — pulmonary nodules stable to minimally decreased, hepatic metastases stable. This represents a best response of stable disease on second-line TKI therapy following prior IO-based induction/maintenance.

2. Hand-foot syndrome (Grade 1 currently, down-graded from Grade 2 at initiation) — well-managed with urea-based cream, cushioned footwear, and lifestyle modifications. No dose reduction needed at this time; patient adapted.

3. Cabozantinib-related diarrhea (Grade 1) — stable on dietary modification and PRN loperamide. No further dose changes warranted at this time.

4. Hypertension — well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. BP 132/82 today. No changes needed.

5. Hypothyroidism (immune-related, from prior ipi/nivo) — stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. TSH 3.1 mIU/L.

6. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 10.8) — chronic, likely multifactorial (single kidney, TKI effect, prior surgery). Stable over multiple checks. No transfusion threshold reached. Iron studies/B12/folate were checked previously and unremarkable. Will continue monitoring.

7. Fatigue and weight loss — multifactorial (disease, TKI, anemia, deconditioning). Patient engaged with integrative oncology for exercise. Palliative care consultation now accepted by patient (see plan below).

---

**PLAN:**

1. Continue cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally daily — no dose change at this visit. This dose has been tolerated for approximately 7 months with reasonable QoL. Disease remains under control.

2. HFS management: Continue urea 40% cream BID to affected areas. Reinforce footwear recommendations. Remind patient to avoid heat exposure and prolonged pressure on feet. No further dermatology referral needed at this time unless worsening.

3. Diarrhea management: Continue PRN loperamide and low-residue dietary guidance. Reinforce adequate oral hydration.

4. Hypertension: Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily. Primary care physician to continue co-managing. Repeat BP check at next visit.

5. Hypothyroidism: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Recheck TFTs in 3 months.

6. Anemia: Monitor CBC at next visit. No intervention at this time.

7. Palliative care consultation — referral placed today. Patient has agreed to meet with the palliative care team for symptom management support, particularly around fatigue, pain from plantar HFS, and psychosocial coping. This is not a hospice discussion — framed clearly as an additional layer of supportive care.

8. Social work: Continue follow-up, especially addressing patient's anxiety around disease status.

9. Urine protein:creatinine ratio — pending, will be reviewed at next visit. If significant proteinuria develops (>1 g/g), cabo dose adjustment may be necessary.

10. Goals of care: Patient continues to express strong desire for active treatment. She understands this is a palliative setting. We discussed that stable disease on TKI represents meaningful benefit and that we will continue cabo as long as it is effective and tolerable. Discussed future scenarios briefly, including what progression would mean in terms of next steps (limited data for third-line after cabo post-IO; clinical trial discussion would be warranted at that time).

11. Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast — in approximately 3 months.

12. Next clinic visit in 4 weeks (routine cabo follow-up/toxicity check), then restaging visit in 3 months.

Time spent: ~45 minutes. Greater than 50% of this visit spent on counseling and coordination of care.

— [Attending Oncologist, MD], Medical Oncology
